title: Factory Physics
keyQuestion: How do we balance the tradeoff between throughput time and capacity when variability is present?
overview: Factory Physics reveals the **fundamental relationships** governing all production systems when variability is present. **Little's Law** ($\text{WIP} = \text{Throughput Rate} \times \text{Throughput Time}$) is the central equation that connects inventory, flow, and time in **any** system — from assembly lines to email inboxes to Disneyland rides. The key insight: **variability degrades performance**, forcing you to choose between holding more inventory (WIP) or accepting reduced throughput. This **"Buffer or Suffer"** principle explains why perfect efficiency (zero inventory) and perfect service (instant flow) can't coexist in the real world.

coreContent.title: Little's Law
coreContent.content:
### The Universal Law of Operations

$$\text{WIP} = \text{Throughput Rate} \times \text{Throughput Time}$$

Or equivalently:

$$\text{Throughput Time} = \frac{\text{WIP}}{\text{Throughput Rate}} \qquad \text{Throughput Rate} = \frac{\text{WIP}}{\text{Throughput Time}}$$

> **You can draw a box around ANY process — or part of a process — and apply Little's Law to whatever is inside the box.** If you know any two variables, you get the third for free.

#### GM Oklahoma City Assembly Plant

| Variable | Value |
|----------|-------|
| Throughput Rate | 72 cars/hour (1 car every 50 seconds) |
| Throughput Time | 28.5 hours per car |
| **WIP** | $72 \times 28.5 = **2{,}052$ cars** in various states of assembly |

#### Why It's Powerful

- **No assumptions required** — works for any stable system regardless of distributions, arrival patterns, or service rules
- **Three variables, know two → get third** — the Swiss Army knife of operations
- **Universal** — factories, hospitals, Starbucks, email inboxes, theme park rides
coreContent.keyInsight: Universal law that applies to any stable system. If you know any two variables, you can calculate the third.

prerequisiteConcepts: C03 (Flow Rate) — throughput rate is the same concept as flow rate from process analysis
C05 (Throughput Time / Flow Time) — time for a job to enter and exit the system
buildsInto: C30 (Queueing Model) — L = λW is Little's Law applied to queues
C23 (WIP-Throughput Tradeoff) — Little's Law quantifies the tradeoff
examAppearances: CC2: Apply Little's Law to calculate WIP, throughput time, or throughput rate
Midterm Q3: Grohe Lux — verify WIP = throughput rate × throughput time before and after changes
Final Review Q1: Better Buzz Coffee — L = λW to find customers in queue

coreContent.title: Visualizing Little's Law
coreContent.content:
### The Pipe Analogy

Imagine a pipe:
- Water flows out at **10 gal./minute**
- Water takes **2 minutes** to move through the pipe
- Amount of water in the pipe = $10 \times 2 = **20$ gallons**

### Dumbo at Disneyland 🎢

| Parameter | Value |
|-----------|-------|
| Seats per ride | 26 people |
| Cycle time | 3 min (2 min ride + 1 min load/unload) |
| Throughput Rate | $26/3 = 8.67$ riders/min |
| **WIP (riders in line)** | **520 riders** |
| **Wait time** | $520 / 8.67 = **60$ minutes** |

> **Exam tip:** When you see "how long is the wait?" or "how many are in the system?" — reach for Little's Law first. It's almost always the fastest path to the answer.
coreContent.keyInsight: Little's Law works everywhere - manufacturing, services, theme parks, email systems. The 'pipe' analogy makes the relationship intuitive.

prerequisiteConcepts: C20 (Little's Law) — direct application of the formula
buildsInto: C21 (WIP) — understanding what WIP means in different contexts
examAppearances: Midterm Q3: Grohe Lux — WIP of 7,500 before, 1,200 after implementation
CC2: Various Little's Law application problems

coreContent.title: Variability Effects
coreContent.content:
### Variability Decreases Throughput Rate

#### Simple Assembly Line Example

- Each station: **average cycle time = 1 hour** (varies 0.5 to 1.5 hours)
- No WIP held between stations
- **Result:** System throughput < 1 unit/hour despite 1-hour averages

#### Why?

- **Blocking:** Station B finishes fast but can't pass work because Station C is still busy
- **Starving:** Station C finishes fast but has nothing to work on because Station B is slow
- Even with **identical average** processing times, variability creates mismatches that waste capacity

> **Key Factory Physics Lesson:** Variability **always** degrades system performance. Systems with more variability perform worse than systems with the same averages but less variability.

#### Implications

- **Reducing variability is free capacity** — you don't need to buy more machines
- **Standardization** (Toyota Production System) reduces variability
- This is why **lean manufacturing** obsesses over consistency
coreContent.keyInsight: Variability always degrades system performance. Even if average processing times are identical, systems with more variability perform worse.

prerequisiteConcepts: C20 (Little's Law) — variability changes the relationship between WIP and throughput
buildsInto: C33 (Variance Effect in Queueing) — same concept quantified with Ca² and Cs²
C23 (WIP-Throughput Tradeoff) — variability forces the tradeoff
examAppearances: Midterm Q3: Grohe Lux — variability explains why reducing WIP hurt throughput
CC2: Variability's role in queueing wait times

coreContent.title: WIP-Throughput Tradeoff
coreContent.content:
### The Fundamental Tradeoff

#### How Throughput Rate Behaves as WIP Increases

- **Low WIP:** Adding more WIP keeps machines busy → throughput rate **rises**
- **At bottleneck capacity:** Throughput rate **plateaus** — the bottleneck can't go faster
- **Beyond:** More WIP just piles up with no throughput benefit

#### How Throughput Time Behaves as WIP Increases

- More WIP **always increases** throughput time (more stuff in the pipe → longer to get through)
- The relationship is roughly **linear** once past the bottleneck capacity

#### The Tradeoff

| Choice | Throughput Rate | Throughput Time | Strategy |
|--------|:---:|:---:|---|
| **Low WIP** | ↓ Lower | ↓ Faster | Prioritize speed/responsiveness |
| **High WIP** | ↑ Higher (to max) | ↑ Slower | Prioritize machine utilization |

> **You cannot optimize both simultaneously when variability exists.** You must choose your operating point based on strategic priorities.

#### Factory Physics Curves

The curves show this visually:
- **Throughput Rate vs. WIP:** Rises then flattens (diminishing returns)
- **Throughput Time vs. WIP:** Rises continuously (no free lunch)
coreContent.keyInsight: More WIP increases throughput rate – up to the bottleneck rate. More WIP increases throughput time. You cannot optimize both simultaneously.

prerequisiteConcepts: C20 (Little's Law) — WIP = Rate × Time connects these three variables
C22 (Variability Effect) — variability is what creates the tradeoff
buildsInto: C24 (Buffer or Suffer) — the principle that resolves the tradeoff
C32 (Utilization Effect) — in queueing, high utilization = high WIP = long waits
examAppearances: Midterm Q3: Grohe Lux — reducing WIP from 7,500 to 1,200 cut throughput time but also cut throughput rate from 1,744 to 1,200
Midterm Q3 Part 2: Explain relationship using Factory Physics curves

coreContent.title: Buffer or Suffer Principle
coreContent.content:
### The No-Free-Lunch Principle

> **"Buffer or Suffer"** — In a system with variability, you **must** hold a WIP buffer or accept reduced throughput. There is no third option.

#### Three Types of Buffers

1. **Inventory buffer** — Hold extra WIP to keep machines fed
2. **Capacity buffer** — Run below max utilization (extra capacity absorbs variability)
3. **Time buffer** — Accept longer lead times

#### Why "Just Eliminate All Inventory" Doesn't Work

- Lean manufacturing ≠ zero inventory
- Toyota uses **controlled, strategic buffers** — not zero buffers
- Grohe Lux slashed WIP → throughput **dropped** because machines starved for work

#### Grohe Lux Case: Buffer or Suffer in Action

| Metric | Before | After |
|--------|:---:|:---:|
| WIP | 7,500 | 1,200 |
| Throughput Time | 4.3 weeks | 1.0 week ✅ |
| Throughput Rate | 1,744 units/week | 1,200 units/week ❌ |

> They removed the buffer and **suffered**: faster flow, but 31% less output = lost revenue.

#### The Strategic Question

Which buffer do you choose? It depends on your **CQDF priorities**:
- Cost-focused → accept time buffer (slower delivery)
- Delivery-focused → hold inventory buffer (higher cost)
- Flexibility-focused → hold capacity buffer (expensive idle resources)
coreContent.keyInsight: Buffer or Suffer - you cannot eliminate all waste without sacrificing performance. This trade-off is fundamental to all operations.

prerequisiteConcepts: C23 (WIP-Throughput Tradeoff) — Buffer or Suffer resolves the tradeoff by accepting one cost
C22 (Variability Effect) — variability is why buffers are necessary
buildsInto: C48 (Safety Stock / Buffers) — inventory buffers in supply chains
C36 (Operations Triangle) — the triangle IS the Buffer or Suffer tradeoff visualized
examAppearances: Midterm Q3: Grohe Lux — "Buffer or Suffer" is the answer to why WIP reduction hurt throughput rate
Midterm Q3 Part 2: Draw Factory Physics curves and explain nonlinear effects

coreContent.title: Factory Physics Applications
coreContent.content:
### Little's Law Across Domains

#### Frank's Factory

| Given | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Lead times (throughput time) | 5 weeks |
| Demand (throughput rate) | 2,000 units/week |
| **WIP** | $5 \times 2{,}000 = **10{,}000$ units** |

#### Drug Store Inventory

| Given | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Sales (throughput rate) | 240 boxes/month |
| Average inventory (WIP) | 80 boxes |
| **Time in stock** | $80/240 = 0.33$ months = **10 days** |

#### Email Inbox

| Given | Value |
|-------|-------|
| Emails requiring response (throughput rate) | 25/day |
| Emails in inbox (WIP) | 75 |
| **Response time** | $75/25 = **3$ days** |

> **The power of Little's Law:** Same formula explains WIP on an assembly line, shelf life of inventory, AND your email response time. The principles are truly universal.
coreContent.keyInsight: Little's Law applies to manufacturing, retail inventory, service processes, and information systems. The principles are universal.

prerequisiteConcepts: C20 (Little's Law) — all examples are direct applications
buildsInto: C30 (Queueing Model) — L = λW is Little's Law for queue length
examAppearances: CC2: Apply Little's Law in various contexts (manufacturing, services)
Midterm Q3: Grohe Lux — verify Little's Law before and after WIP changes

formulas.name: Little's Law

variables.WIP: Work in Process (units currently in system)
variables.Throughput Time: Time a unit spends in the system

formulas.workedExample: GM Assembly: WIP = 72 cars/hr × 28.5 hours = **2,052 cars** in process

formulas.name: Throughput Time Calculation
formulas.workedExample: Drug store: 80 boxes ÷ 240 boxes/month = **0.33 months** = 10 days per box

practiceProblems.question:
### Frank's Factory WIP Estimation

Frank's factory has **lead times averaging 5 weeks** and **demand of 2,000 units/week**.

Three people estimate WIP:
- **Pat:** 12,000–13,000 units
- **Marcia:** 10,000 units
- **Machine operator:** 5,000 units

**Who should Frank believe, and why?**
practiceProblems.solution:
### Solution

Using Little's Law:

$$\text{WIP} = \text{Throughput Rate} \times \text{Throughput Time}$$

$$\text{WIP} = 2{,}000 \text{ units/week} \times 5 \text{ weeks} = **10{,}000 \text{ units}**$$

**Frank should believe Marcia.** Little's Law provides the mathematically correct answer based on the given flow rate and lead time. No need to physically count — the law guarantees this relationship for any stable system.

> Pat overestimates (perhaps counting finished goods too), the operator underestimates (perhaps only seeing their station).
practiceProblems.source: Factory Physics Practice Problems and Solutions.json

practiceProblems.question:
### Department Store Customers

About **220 customers** enter a department store per hour, and customers stay **45 minutes** on average.

**What is the average number of customers in the store?**
practiceProblems.solution:
### Solution

$$\text{WIP} = \text{Throughput Rate} \times \text{Throughput Time}$$

- Throughput Rate = 220 customers/hour
- Throughput Time = 45 minutes = **0.75 hours**

$$\text{WIP} = 220 \times 0.75 = **165 \text{ customers}**$$

> **Watch the units!** Convert 45 minutes to 0.75 hours to match the throughput rate units. Unit mismatch is the #1 Little's Law error on exams.
practiceProblems.source: Factory Physics Practice Problems and Solutions.json

practiceProblems.question:
### Email Response Time

You receive **25 emails per day** requiring response, with **75 emails** in your inbox on average.

**How long on average does it take you to respond to an email?**
practiceProblems.solution:
### Solution

$$\text{Throughput Time} = \frac{\text{WIP}}{\text{Throughput Rate}} = \frac{75}{25} = **3 \text{ days}**$$

> On average it takes **3 days** to respond. Want to cut response time to 1 day? Either reduce inbox WIP to 25 emails (triage/delete faster) or increase throughput rate to 75 emails/day (not realistic). Little's Law shows the levers clearly.
practiceProblems.source: Factory Physics Practice Problems and Solutions.json

practiceProblems.question:
### Dumbo Ride at Disneyland 🎢

Dumbo seats **26 people per ride**, with a **2-minute ride + 1-minute loading/unloading = 3-minute cycle**.

If **520 passengers** are in line, what wait time should Disney post?
practiceProblems.solution:
### Solution

**Step 1:** Calculate throughput rate
$$\text{Throughput Rate} = \frac{26 \text{ riders}}{3 \text{ min}} = 8.67 \text{ riders/min}$$

**Step 2:** Apply Little's Law (solve for throughput time)
$$\text{Wait Time} = \frac{\text{WIP}}{\text{Throughput Rate}} = \frac{520}{8.67} = **60 \text{ minutes}**$$

Disney should post a **60-minute wait time**.

> **Fun fact:** Disney actually overestimates posted wait times slightly, so guests are pleasantly surprised when the actual wait is shorter (Psychology of Waiting principle #4: uncertain waits feel longer than known waits).
practiceProblems.source: Factory Physics After Class Handouts Haas 2026.json

keyInsights: Three things that hurt you in operations: **Setups, Bottlenecks, Uncertainty & Variability**
The **bottleneck** defines the maximum throughput rate of the system
More WIP increases throughput rate — **up to the bottleneck rate**
WIP management balances the tradeoff between throughput time and capacity
**Buffer or Suffer** — you must hold WIP buffer or accept reduced throughput
Little's Law is **universal** — applies to any stable system with flow
